questions.prompt: Why hide critical actions ONLY behind a custom gesture?

choices.text: Don't — discoverability disaster. Gestures are powerful but INVISIBLE; users can't see what they don't know exists. Critical actions hidden behind only-gesture access mean users who don't discover the gesture can never reach the feature; they don't even know it exists to look up. Apple itself has this problem (some hidden Safari / Photos gestures are notoriously undiscoverable). Pattern: critical actions should have a tap-accessible path (a visible button, menu item, or context menu) AS WELL AS the gesture shortcut for power users. Example: deleting a list item — visible 'Edit' button reveals delete buttons + swipe gesture deletes faster for those who know. Both paths exist; gesture is shortcut, not gate. Plus accessibility — users with Voice Control / Switch Control can't perform gestures; need button equivalent. The rule: gestures ENHANCE the experience; they shouldn't be the ONLY way to access anything important. Test by removing gesture awareness mentally — can users still accomplish core tasks? If not, the design is broken.

questions.explanation: Gestures are invisible; users can't discover what they don't know exists. Always provide tap alternative.

questions.prompt: Designer made tap targets 24×24pt to look cleaner. Production users report mistaps. Fix?

choices.text: Expand tap area via `.contentShape` while keeping 24pt visual. SwiftUI: `Image('icon').frame(width: 24, height: 24).contentShape(Rectangle().size(width: 44, height: 44)).onTapGesture { ... }` — visible 24pt icon, 44pt invisible tap area, both designer aesthetic AND HIG ergonomics. UIKit equivalent: hitTest extends beyond visible bounds; or use a UIButton with image inside (button has full hit area, image is sized smaller). The cleanest visual + meeting the human floor for fingertip ergonomics (~10-14mm contact patch needs ~44pt target). 44pt is non-negotiable for touch; cleaner-looking 24pt tap areas cause real-world mistaps, frustrated users, 1-star reviews. The trade-off doesn't exist: you can have both via the technique. Apple does this routinely — many of their visible icons are smaller than the actual hit area. Designers who insist 'small targets look better' need to be shown the technique; usually 'oh, that works' resolves the disagreement.

questions.explanation: Expand hit area via .contentShape; keep 24pt visual. 24 visible / 44 tappable = best of both.
